questions.specification: A researcher is studying the distribution of blood types in a population. According to national statistics, the expected distribution of blood types is: Type O (44%), Type A (42%), Type B (10%), and Type AB (4%). In a sample of 200 people from a specific region, the following frequencies were observed: Type O: 75, Type A: 92, Type B: 24, Type AB: 9.

parts.content: State the null and alternative hypotheses for this test.
parts.markscheme:
- State null hypothesis: $H_0$: The distribution of blood types in the region follows the national distribution OR $H_0: p_1 = 0.44, p_2 = 0.42, p_3 = 0.10, p_4 = 0.04$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State alternative hypothesis: $H_1$: The distribution of blood types in the region differs from the national distribution OR $H_1$: At least one of these proportions is different <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept either form of stating hypotheses. Both must be correctly stated for full marks.</note>

steps.title: Identify the test type
steps.content:
In this problem, we are comparing the observed frequencies of blood types in a specific region to the expected frequencies based on **national statistics**. This is known as a **Chi-squared Goodness of Fit test**. 

You can find more about this in **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability (SL 4.11)**. Before we do any math, we must define our starting assumptions, which are the null and alternative hypotheses.

steps.title: Define the null hypothesis
steps.content:
The **null hypothesis** ($H_0$) represents the "default" position or the assumption that the data fits the expected model perfectly. 

Using the Descriptive Statement approach, we write this as a full sentence stating that the regional data matches the national data:

$$H_0: \text{The distribution of blood types in the region follows the national distribution.}$$

steps.title: Define the alternative hypothesis
steps.content:
The **alternative hypothesis** ($H_1$) is the opposite of the null hypothesis. It claims that the observed data does not fit the expected model. 

We state this as:

$$H_1: \text{The distribution of blood types in the region does not follow the national distribution.}$$ 

(Note: You can also say it "differs from" the national distribution. Both phrases earn the mark!)

steps.title: Identify the parameters
steps.content:
In a Chi-squared goodness of fit test, we use mathematical parameters ($p$) to represent the expected proportions for each category. Based on the national statistics provided, we can assign the following proportions:

*   $p_1$: Proportion of Type O
*   $p_2$: Proportion of Type A
*   $p_3$: Proportion of Type B
*   $p_4$: Proportion of Type AB

highlights.text: Type O (44%), Type A (42%), Type B (10%), and Type AB (4%)

steps.title: State the Null Hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis ($H_0$) represents the claim that the observed regional distribution matches the national distribution exactly. Using the percentages converted to decimals:

$$H_0: p_1 = 0.44, p_2 = 0.42, p_3 = 0.10, p_4 = 0.04$$

steps.title: State the Alternative Hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis ($H_1$) is the opposite of the null. It suggests that the regional distribution does not follow the national one. In mathematical terms, we state that at least one of the proportions is different from the null hypothesis:

$$H_1: \text{At least one proportion is different}$$

steps.title: Final check of hypotheses
steps.content:
To earn both marks, ensure you have correctly listed both $H_0$ and $H_1$. This topic is covered in **Topic 4.11** (Chi-squared goodness of fit tests) of your syllabus.

$$\begin{aligned} H_0: & p_1 = 0.44, p_2 = 0.42, p_3 = 0.10, p_4 = 0.04 \\ H_1: & \text{At least one } p_i \text{ is different} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Calculate the expected frequencies for each blood type.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate expected frequencies for Type O and Type A: $200 \times 0.44 = 88$ and $200 \times 0.42 = 84$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate expected frequencies for Type B and Type AB: $200 \times 0.10 = 20$ and $200 \times 0.04 = 8$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for each correct calculation of two expected frequencies.</note>

steps.title: Identify the expected frequency formula
steps.content:
To find the expected frequency for each blood type, we multiply the total sample size by the probability (percentage) of that specific type. In probability theory, this is the same as finding the expected value $E(X)$.

We are given a total sample size of **200 people**.

steps.title: Calculate Type O and A
steps.content:
First, convert the percentages to decimals: 44% becomes $0.44$ and 42% becomes $0.42$. 

For Type O:
$$E_O = 200 \times 0.44 = 88$$

For Type A:
$$E_A = 200 \times 0.42 = 84$$

steps.title: Calculate Type B and AB
steps.content:
Next, we perform the same steps for the remaining two blood types: 10% becomes $0.10$ and 4% becomes $0.04$.

For Type B:
$$E_B = 200 \times 0.10 = 20$$

For Type AB:
$$E_{AB} = 200 \times 0.04 = 8$$

steps.title: Verify with Technology
steps.content: You can quickly perform these multiplications on your calculator to ensure accuracy. A good way to check your work is to ensure the sum of all expected frequencies equals the total sample size: $88 + 84 + 20 + 8 = 200$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the probabilities into List 1 and multiply the entire list by 200 to get all results at once.

ti84.instructions: On the home screen, type the multiplication directly for each blood type.

desmos.instructions: Type the arithmetic expressions into the input bars.

calculator.description: Calculate expected frequencies on a GDC.

steps.title: State final frequencies
steps.content:
The expected frequencies are:
- Type O: **88**
- Type A: **84**
- Type B: **20**
- Type AB: **8**

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for the first two correct values and **1 mark** for the last two.

steps.title: Identify the key information
steps.content:
To calculate the expected frequencies, we need two pieces of information: the total sample size ($n$) and the expected distribution (probabilities) for each blood type. 

From the question, we have:
- Total sample size: $n = 200$
- Expected percentages: Type O (44%), Type A (42%), Type B (10%), and Type AB (4%).

highlights.text: Type O (44%), Type A (42%), Type B (10%), and Type AB (4%)

steps.title: State the expected value formula
steps.content:
In a Goodness of Fit test, the expected frequency for each category is calculated by multiplying the total sample size by the probability of that category occurring. This follows the formula for the expected value of a binomial distribution found in your data booklet:

$$\mathrm{E}(X) = np$$

steps.title: Input data into GDC lists
steps.content:
Using the **GDC List Operation** approach, we first input the expected probabilities (converted from percentages to decimals) into a list. 

- Type O: $0.44$
- Type A: $0.42$
- Type B: $0.10$
- Type AB: $0.04$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Spreadsheet/List menu. Enter 0.44, 0.42, 0.1, and 0.04 into column A (or List 1).

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] then [ENTER] for Edit. In L1, enter 0.44, 0.42, 0.10, and 0.04.

calculator.description: Enter the probabilities into List 1.

steps.title: Perform the list operation
steps.content: Now, we define a second list ($L_2$) as the product of our total sample size ($200$) and the probabilities in List 1. This will generate all expected frequencies simultaneously.

gdc.instructions: In the header of column B (or List 2), type the formula: = 200 * List1 (or A).

ti84.instructions: Scroll up to highlight the header 'L2' and press [ENTER]. Type 200 * L1 (press [2nd] [1] for L1) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Calculate the expected list: L_2 = 200 * L_1

calculator.description: Calculate expected frequencies in List 2.

steps.title: State final expected frequencies
steps.content:
Based on the list operations, the expected frequencies ($f_e$) for each blood type are:

- **Type O**: $200 \times 0.44 = 88$
- **Type A**: $200 \times 0.42 = 84$
- **Type B**: $200 \times 0.10 = 20$
- **Type AB**: $200 \times 0.04 = 8$

These values are used to compare against the observed frequencies in a chi-squared test.

steps.title: Identify total sample size
steps.content: First, we identify the total number of people in the study from the question data. This is our sample size, $n = 200$.

steps.title: Determine scaling factor
steps.content:
Percentages represent counts per 100. Since our sample size is 200, which is exactly double 100, we can use a scaling factor of 2. We will simply multiply each given percentage value by this factor to find the expected counts for 200 people.

$$\text{Scaling Factor} = \frac{200}{100} = 2$$

steps.title: Calculate Type O and A
steps.content:
Using our scaling factor of 2, we multiply the given percentage values for Type O and Type A. This matches the formula for expected value from the data booklet:

$$E(X) = np$$

- **Expected Type O**: $44 \times 2 = 88$
- **Expected Type A**: $42 \times 2 = 84$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the total sample (200) by the decimal form of the percentages.

ti84.instructions: In the calculation screen, enter 200 * 0.44 and 200 * 0.42.

desmos.instructions: Enter the multiplications into the expression bars.

calculator.description: Calculate expected frequencies for Type O and Type A by multiplying the sample size by the probability (decimal).

steps.title: Calculate Type B and AB
steps.content:
We repeat the process for Type B and Type AB by doubling their respective percentage values:

- **Expected Type B**: $10 \times 2 = 20$
- **Expected Type AB**: $4 \times 2 = 8$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the total sample by the decimal form of the percentages.

ti84.instructions: In the calculation screen, enter 200 * 0.10 and 200 * 0.04.

desmos.instructions: Enter the multiplications into the expression bars.

calculator.description: Calculate expected frequencies for Type B and Type AB.

parts.content: Calculate the chi-squared test statistic. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute observed and expected values into the formula: $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(f_o - f_e)^2}{f_e}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate components correctly: Type O: $\frac{(75-88)^2}{88} \approx 1.920$, Type A: $\frac{(92-84)^2}{84} \approx 0.762$, Type B: $\frac{(24-20)^2}{20} = 0.800$, Type AB: $\frac{(9-8)^2}{8} = 0.125$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the final test statistic: $\chi^2 = 1.920 + 0.762 + 0.800 + 0.125 = 3.607$ (accept 3.61) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award partial marks for correct method with arithmetic errors.</note>

steps.title: State the Chi-Squared formula
steps.content:
To find the chi-squared test statistic, we use the formula for the goodness of fit test from your data booklet:

$$\chi^{2} = \sum \frac{(f_{o}-f_{e})^{2}}{f_{e}}$$

where $f_{o}$ represents the observed frequencies and $f_{e}$ represents the expected frequencies.

steps.title: Calculate expected frequencies
steps.content:
First, we need to calculate the expected frequency ($f_{e}$) for each blood type. We do this by multiplying the total sample size of **200 people** by the expected percentage for each category:

- **Type O:** $200 \times 0.44 = 88$
- **Type A:** $200 \times 0.42 = 84$
- **Type B:** $200 \times 0.10 = 20$
- **Type AB:** $200 \times 0.04 = 8$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the total sample size by each probability.

desmos.instructions: Type the multiplications into the expression bars.

calculator.description: Calculate each expected frequency by multiplying the total (200) by the decimal form of the percentage.

highlights.text: Type O (44%), Type A (42%), Type B (10%), and Type AB (4%)

steps.title: Calculate chi-squared components
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the observed frequencies ($f_{o}$) and the expected frequencies ($f_{e}$) we just found into the component part of the formula, $\frac{(f_{o}-f_{e})^{2}}{f_{e}}$, for each blood type:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Type O:} & \quad \frac{(75-88)^{2}}{88} = \frac{(-13)^{2}}{88} = \frac{169}{88} \approx 1.920 \\ \text{Type A:} & \quad \frac{(92-84)^{2}}{84} = \frac{8^{2}}{84} = \frac{64}{84} \approx 0.762 \\ \text{Type B:} & \quad \frac{(24-20)^{2}}{20} = \frac{4^{2}}{20} = \frac{16}{20} = 0.800 \\ \text{Type AB:} & \quad \frac{(9-8)^{2}}{8} = \frac{1^{2}}{8} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate each fraction separately.

desmos.instructions: Enter each fraction to find the decimal values.

calculator.description: Calculate each individual component of the chi-squared sum.

highlights.text: Type O: 75, Type A: 92, Type B: 24, Type AB: 9

steps.title: Sum the components
steps.content:
Finally, we sum these individual components to find the total chi-squared test statistic:

$$\chi^{2} = 1.920 + 0.762 + 0.800 + 0.125 = 3.607$$

Rounding to 3 decimal places (or the standard 3 significant figures), we get **3.61**.

gdc.instructions: Sum all four components.

calculator.description: Sum the calculated components to get the final test statistic.

steps.title: Calculate expected frequencies
steps.content:
To perform a Chi-squared Goodness of Fit test, we first need the expected frequencies ($f_e$) for each blood type. We calculate these by multiplying the national percentages by the total sample size of $200$.

- **Type O**: $0.44 \times 200 = 88$
- **Type A**: $0.42 \times 200 = 84$
- **Type B**: $0.10 \times 200 = 20$
- **Type AB**: $0.04 \times 200 = 8$

These values are essential for the formula and for entering data into your GDC.

gdc.instructions: Perform basic multiplication for each percentage and the total sample size (200).

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.44 * 200, then 0.42 * 200, etc., on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expressions into separate lines.

calculator.description: Calculate the expected values for each category.

steps.title: Apply the $\chi^2$ formula
steps.content:
Although we will use the GDC to find the final value, the markscheme requires showing the substitution into the chi-squared formula:

$$\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(f_o - f_e)^2}{f_e}$$

We substitute our observed values ($f_o$) and our calculated expected values ($f_e$) for each group.

steps.title: Calculate component values
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the contribution of each blood type to the total $\chi^2$ statistic. This step is worth an **accuracy mark (A1)** in the exam.

- **Type O**: $\frac{(75-88)^2}{88} \approx 1.920$
- **Type A**: $\frac{(92-84)^2}{84} \approx 0.762$
- **Type B**: $\frac{(24-20)^2}{20} = 0.800$
- **Type AB**: $\frac{(9-8)^2}{8} = 0.125$

Be careful with rounding here; keep at least 3 decimal places for accuracy.

gdc.instructions: Calculate (observed - expected)^2 / expected for each category.

ti84.instructions: Enter (75-88)^2 / 88 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Use the formula for each pair of values.

calculator.description: Calculate each component of the chi-squared sum.

highlights.text: Type O: 75, Type A: 92, Type B: 24, Type AB: 9

steps.title: Find the test statistic
steps.content:
Finally, we sum these components to find the total test statistic:

$$\chi^2 = 1.920 + 0.762 + 0.800 + 0.125 = 3.607$$

You can also find this directly using the GDC's built-in **$\chi^2$ GOF-Test** function, which is a great way to verify your manual working.

gdc.instructions: Enter observed values in List 1 and expected values in List 2. Use the 'Chi-squared Goodness of Fit' test function. Set the degrees of freedom (df) to 3.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT], select [EDIT], and enter observed values in L1 and expected values in L2.
2. Press [STAT], scroll to [TESTS], and select [D:χ2 GOF-Test].
3. Set Observed: L1, Expected: L2, and df: 3 (since there are 4 categories, df = 4 - 1).
4. Select [Calculate].

desmos.instructions: Sum the previously calculated components.

parts.content: Using a 5% significance level, state the critical value for this test and make a conclusion.
parts.markscheme:
- Degrees of freedom = $4 - 1 = 3$

- State critical value at 5% significance level: $7.815$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Compare test statistic to critical value: $3.607 < 7.815$ therefore fail to reject $H_0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State conclusion in context: Insufficient evidence to suggest that the distribution of blood types in this region differs from the national distribution <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award full marks for equivalent valid conclusions that reference the context.</note>

questions.specification:
A botanist is investigating the inheritance of flower color in a specific hybrid plant. They hypothesize that the distribution of colors follows a ratio of $9:3:3:1$ for Purple, Pink, White, and Variegated flowers respectively. A sample of 160 plants is grown and the observed frequencies are recorded in the following table.

| Flower Color | Purple | Pink | White | Variegated |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Observed frequency | 85 | 35 | 28 | 12 |

A chi-squared goodness of fit test is performed at a $1\%$ significance level to determine whether the botanist's hypothesis is consistent with the observed results.

1 mark total

questions.specification:
A researcher tests whether a new fertilizer affects the number of flowers produced by a plant species, assumed to follow a Poisson distribution. Data from 200 plants are summarized:

| Flowers | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 or more |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Frequency | 25 | 50 | 60 | 45 | 20 |

A $\chi^{2}$ goodness of fit test is performed at the $5\%$ significance level, with the Poisson mean estimated from the data.

parts.content: Estimate the Poisson mean using the sample data.
parts.markscheme:
Total flowers $= 0 \times 25 + 1 \times 50 + 2 \times 60 + 3 \times 45 + 4 \times 20 = 385$ <marks>M1</marks>

Sample mean $= \frac{385}{200}$ <marks>A1</marks>

$= 1.925$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the estimation method
steps.content:
To estimate the Poisson mean ($ \lambda $) from a frequency table, we calculate the sample mean ($ \bar{x} $). 

We will use the formula for the mean of a frequency distribution from your data booklet:

$$\bar{x}=\frac{\sum f_{i}x_{i}}{n}$$

Where $ x_i $ represents the number of flowers, $ f_i $ is the frequency for each count, and $ n $ is the total number of plants.

steps.title: Calculate total number of flowers
steps.content:
First, we find the sum of all flowers produced by the 200 plants. For the final category "4 or more", we use the value 4 as our best estimate for the calculation.

$$\text{Total flowers} = (0 \times 25) + (1 \times 50) + (2 \times 60) + (3 \times 45) + (4 \times 20)$$

Using manual calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 \times 25 &= 0 \\ 1 \times 50 &= 50 \\ 2 \times 60 &= 120 \\ 3 \times 45 &= 135 \\ 4 \times 20 &= 80 \end{aligned}$$

Summing these together:

$$0 + 50 + 120 + 135 + 80 = 385$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression on the home screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of flowers.

steps.title: Divide by total plants
steps.content:
Now, divide the total number of flowers by the total number of plants ($ n = 200 $) to find the mean.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{385}{200}$$

Calculating this gives:

$$\bar{x} = 1.925$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 385 by 200.

ti84.instructions: Divide 385 by 200.

desmos.instructions: Divide 385 by 200.

calculator.description: Divide the total flowers by the sample size.

steps.title: State the final estimate
steps.content:
The estimate for the Poisson mean, based on the sample data, is $1.925$.

In an IB exam, you earn marks for:
- Correctly calculating the total number of flowers (**M1**).
- Setting up the division by the total frequency (**A1**).
- Providing the final correct numerical value (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify data and frequencies
steps.content:
To estimate the Poisson mean ($\lambda$), we need to find the sample mean ($\bar{x}$) of the data. We have the number of flowers ($x$) and their frequencies ($f$). Note that for the "4 or more" category, we use the value 4 as per the standard estimation method for this problem.

Here is our data set:
*   **Values ($x$):** $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$
*   **Frequencies ($f$):** $25, 50, 60, 45, 20$

steps.title: Input data into GDC
steps.content:
On your graphing calculator, enter the flower counts into one list (often $L_1$) and the frequencies into a second list (often $L_2$). This allows the calculator to perform a weighted average calculation based on the formula for the mean of a frequency distribution:

$$\bar{x}=\frac{\sum f_{i}x_{i}}{n}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Statistics menu and select List Editor.
2. In List 1, enter: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
3. In List 2, enter: 25, 50, 60, 45, 20.
4. Navigate to CALC or F6, then 1-Variable.
5. Ensure the settings are: XList: List 1 and Frequency: List 2.
6. View the results.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] and select 1:Edit.
2. Enter 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 into L1.
3. Enter 25, 50, 60, 45, 20 into L2.
4. Press [STAT], arrow right to CALC, and select 1:1-Var Stats.
5. Set List: L1 and FreqList: L2.
6. Select Calculate and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Create a list for values: v = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4].
2. Create a list for frequencies: f = [25, 50, 60, 45, 20].
3. Calculate the total count: n = total(f).
4. Calculate the weighted mean: mean = total(v * f) / n.

calculator.description: Input the data into lists and calculate 1-variable statistics.

steps.title: Find the sample mean
steps.content:
Once you run the **1-Var Stats** command, look for the symbol $ \bar{x} $, which represents the sample mean. 

From the calculator output:

$$\bar{x} = 1.925$$

This value is the estimate for the Poisson mean, $ \lambda $.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The estimate for the Poisson mean using the sample data is $1.925$.

In an IB exam, showing the manual calculation setup can help secure method marks:

$$\text{Mean} = \frac{(0 \times 25) + (1 \times 50) + (2 \times 60) + (3 \times 45) + (4 \times 20)}{200} = 1.925$$

parts.content: Calculate the expected frequency for 1 flower, using the estimated mean.
parts.markscheme:
For $X \sim \text{Po}(1.925)$, $P(X=1) = e^{-1.925} \times 1.925$ <marks>M1</marks>

$P(X=1) \approx 0.28081...$ <marks>A1</marks>

Expected frequency $= 0.28081... \times 200 \approx 56.16$ (accept $56.2$) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the estimated mean
steps.content:
To find the expected frequency, we first need the estimated mean ($\lambda$ or $m$) of the Poisson distribution. This is calculated from the weighted frequency table by dividing the total number of flowers by the total number of plants.

$$\lambda = \frac{(0 \times 25) + (1 \times 50) + (2 \times 60) + (3 \times 45) + (4 \times 20)}{200} = \frac{385}{200} = 1.925$$

highlights.text: Poisson mean estimated from the data

steps.title: Apply the Poisson formula
steps.content:
Now, we use the Poisson probability mass function formula to find the probability of a plant having exactly **1 flower** ($ x = 1 $). The formula for a Poisson distribution $ X \sim \text{Po}(m) $ is:

$$P(X=x) = \frac{e^{-m} m^x}{x!}$$

Substituting $ m = 1.925 $ and $ x = 1 $:

$$P(X=1) = \frac{e^{-1.925} \times 1.925^1}{1!}$$

Using a calculator, we find:

$$P(X=1) \approx 0.28081...$$

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the Probability or Distribution menu, select Poisson PDF, and input the mean (1.925) and the x-value (1).

ti84.instructions: Go to [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR). Select 'poissonpdf('. Enter λ: 1.925, x value: 1. Press Paste and ENTER.

steps.title: Calculate expected frequency
steps.content:
The expected frequency is the probability of the event occurring multiplied by the total number of trials (the total sample size $n=200$).

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected Frequency} &= P(X=1) \times 200 \\ \text{Expected Frequency} &= 0.28081... \times 200 \\ \text{Expected Frequency} &\approx 56.162... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get **56.2**.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the calculated probability by 200 in the next row.

steps.title: Estimate the Poisson mean
steps.content:
To calculate expected frequencies for a Poisson distribution, we first need to estimate the mean ($\lambda$) from the observed data. We use the formula for the weighted mean:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_{i}x_{i}}{n}$$

Based on the table, we treat the "4 or more" category as exactly 4 for the purpose of this calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{(0 \times 25) + (1 \times 50) + (2 \times 60) + (3 \times 45) + (4 \times 20)}{200} \\ \lambda &= \frac{0 + 50 + 120 + 135 + 80}{200} = \frac{385}{200} = 1.925 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the mean by dividing the total number of flowers by the total number of plants.

steps.title: Find the probability P(X=1)
steps.content:
Now we use the **Poisson probability density function (poissonPdf)** on the calculator to find the probability of a plant having exactly 1 flower, given our estimated mean $\lambda = 1.925$.

$$P(X=1) = \frac{e^{-1.925} \times 1.925^1}{1!} \approx 0.28081...$$

steps.title: Calculate the expected frequency
steps.content:
The expected frequency for 1 flower is found by multiplying the total number of plants ($n = 200$) by the probability we just calculated:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected Frequency} &= n \times P(X=1) \\ \text{Expected Frequency} &= 200 \times 0.28081... \\ \text{Expected Frequency} &\approx 56.16 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final result
steps.content:
The expected frequency for 1 flower is approximately **$56.2$** (to 3 significant figures).

In an IB exam, marks are awarded for:
1. Setting up the probability calculation using the mean (M1).
2. Finding the correct probability (A1).
3. Calculating the final expected frequency (A1).

parts.content: Given the $\chi^{2}$ test statistic is 6.35 with 3 degrees of freedom, state the conclusion of the test. Justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
Critical value for $\chi^{2}$ with 3 df at $5\% = 7.815$ <marks>A1</marks>

Since $6.35 < 7.815$, do not reject $H_{0}$ <marks>M1</marks>

The data can be modeled by a Poisson distribution at the $5\%$ significance level. <marks>A1</marks>

Note: Accept conclusion based on $p$-value $> 0.05$.

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To determine the conclusion of our test using the **Critical Value Comparison** approach, we first need to identify the key parameters provided in the problem:

*   **Test Statistic ($\chi^2_{calc}$):** $6.35$
*   **Degrees of Freedom ($df$):** $3$
*   **Significance Level ($\alpha$):** $0.05$ (or $5\%$)

Our goal is to find the 'boundary' value (critical value) that separates the region where we keep the null hypothesis from the region where we reject it.

steps.title: Find the critical value
steps.content:
Using a $ \chi^2 $ distribution table or your GDC, we find the critical value for $ 3 $ degrees of freedom at a $ 5\% $ significance level. 

This is the value $ k $ such that $ P(\chi^2 > k) = 0.05 $, which is equivalent to saying the cumulative area to the left is $ 0.95 $.

$$\chi^2_{crit} (0.05, 3) = 7.815$$

gdc.instructions: In the Statistics menu, look for Distributions -> Chi-squared -> Inverse. Enter the area (0.95) and the degrees of freedom (3).

ti84.instructions: Go to [2nd] [VARS] (DISTRI), select 'invNorm' is for normal, but for Chi-squared you usually look up a table or use a specific solver. On newer TI-84 Plus CE models: [2nd] [VARS], select 'invChi2'. Enter Area: 0.95, df: 3.

desmos.instructions: Use the inverse cumulative probability function for the chi-squared distribution.

calculator.description: Find the critical value for a Chi-squared distribution with df=3 and alpha=0.05.

steps.title: Compare and decide
steps.content:
Now we compare our calculated test statistic to the critical value. The decision rule for a chi-squared test is:

*   If $\chi^2_{calc} > \chi^2_{crit}$, reject $H_0$.
*   If $\chi^2_{calc} \leq \chi^2_{crit}$, do **not** reject $H_0$.

In our case:
$$6.35 < 7.815$$

Because the test statistic is less than the critical value, it does not fall into the 'rejection region' (the tail of the distribution).

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Since we do not reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$), we conclude that there is insufficient evidence to suggest the data does not follow the assumed distribution.

**Conclusion:** The data can be modeled by a Poisson distribution at the $5\%$ significance level.

steps.title: Calculate the p-value
steps.content:
To determine the conclusion of the test using the $ p $-value approach, we first need to find the $ p $-value corresponding to our $ \chi^2 $ test statistic. 

The $ p $-value is the probability that a $ \chi^2 $ distribution with $ 3 $ degrees of freedom takes a value greater than or equal to our test statistic, $ 6.35 $. 

$$p = P(\chi^2 > 6.35) \text{ with } df = 3$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, choose Distributions, then Chi-squared, then Cumulative (CD). Set the lower bound to 6.35, the upper bound to a very large number (like 9999), and degrees of freedom to 3.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select '8: $\chi^2$cdf('. Enter 'Lower: 6.35', 'Upper: 1E99', and 'df: 3'. Select 'Paste' and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type `1 - chi2dist(3).cdf(6.35)` to find the area to the right of 6.35 for 3 degrees of freedom.

calculator.description: Calculate the $p$-value using the Chi-squared cumulative distribution function.

steps.title: Compare with significance level
steps.content:
From the calculator, we find the $p$-value is approximately:

$$p \approx 0.0958$$

Now, we compare this $p$-value to the significance level, $\alpha = 0.05$. 

In a hypothesis test, the decision rule is:
- If $p < \alpha$, reject $H_0$.
- If $p \geq \alpha$, do not reject $H_0$.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since $0.0958 > 0.05$, the $p$-value is greater than the significance level. Therefore, we do **not reject** the null hypothesis ($H_0$).

**Conclusion:** At the $5\%$ significance level, there is insufficient evidence to suggest the data does not follow a Poisson distribution. We conclude that the data can be modeled by a Poisson distribution.

- Group Y: mean $\bar{x}_Y = 22$ minutes, standard deviation $s_Y = 3.5$ minutes, sample size $n_Y = 21$.

The researcher conducts a two-sample $t$-test at the $1\%$ significance level to determine if there is a difference in the mean completion times between the two groups.

steps.title: Define population parameters
steps.content:
Before stating our hypotheses, we must define the parameters we are testing. In a two-sample $t$-test, we are comparing the means of two distinct populations. 

Let:
- $\mu_X$ be the population mean completion time for **Group X**.
- $\mu_Y$ be the population mean completion time for **Group Y**.

steps.title: State the null hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis, denoted by $H_0$, is the statement that there is no effect or no difference between the two groups. 

For this test, we assume the mean completion times for both groups are equal:
$$H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$$

steps.title: State the alternative hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis, denoted by $H_1$, is what the researcher is looking for evidence of. 

The question asks to determine if there is a **difference** in the means. Since it does not specify a direction (e.g., "greater than" or "less than"), we use a two-tailed approach:
$$H_1: \mu_X \neq \mu_Y$$

highlights.text: difference in the mean completion times

steps.title: Identify the test type
steps.content:
The researcher is comparing the mean completion times of two independent groups, Group X and Group Y. To determine if a difference exists, we conduct a two-sample $t$-test. This process begins by defining the null and alternative hypotheses. 

This topic is covered in **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability** (specifically section 4.11), which focuses on formulating hypotheses for various statistical tests.

highlights.text: difference in the mean completion times between the two groups

steps.title: Define the null hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis ($H_0$) represents the "no change" or "no effect" position. In the **Verbal Description** approach, we state that there is no difference between the population parameters being compared.

**Verbal statement:** There is no difference in the mean completion times between Group X and Group Y.

**Mathematical statement:**
$$H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$$

steps.title: Define the alternative hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis ($H_1$) represents the claim that we are looking for evidence to support. Since the question asks if there is simply "a difference" (rather than asking if one specific group is faster than the other), we use a two-tailed statement.

**Verbal statement:** There is a difference in the mean completion times between Group X and Group Y.

**Mathematical statement:**
$$H_1: \mu_X \neq \mu_Y$$

highlights.text: determine if there is a difference

steps.title: Verify markscheme requirements
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure you clearly distinguish between $H_0$ and $H_1$ and use the correct population mean symbols ($\mu$).

- **A1** is awarded for stating the null hypothesis correctly ($H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$).
- **A1** is awarded for stating the alternative hypothesis correctly ($H_1: \mu_X \neq \mu_Y$).

parts.content: Explain what is meant by a Type I error in this context.
parts.markscheme:
- Define a Type I error as rejecting the null hypothesis $H_0$ when it is actually true <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain in context: concluding that there is a difference in the mean completion times between Group X and Group Y when the means are actually equal <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define Type I error
steps.content: In statistics, a **Type I error** occurs when we reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$) even though it is actually true. You can think of this as a "false alarm" or a "false positive."

steps.title: Identify the null hypothesis
steps.content:
For this two-sample $t$-test, the null hypothesis ($H_0$) is the statement of "no difference." In this context, it is:

$$H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$$

This means that the true mean completion time for Group X is equal to the true mean completion time for Group Y.

steps.title: Apply to the context
steps.content:
To explain this in context, we combine the definition with the specific $H_0$ we identified. 

A Type I error occurs if the researcher concludes there **is a difference** in the mean completion times between Group X and Group Y, when in reality, the **means are actually equal**.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, ensure you include both the general definition and the specific application:

1.  **General**: Rejecting $H_0$ when it is true.
2.  **Context**: Concluding there is a difference in mean times between Group X and Group Y when there is actually no difference.

steps.title: Define Type I error
steps.content:
In statistics, a Type I error is often described as a "false positive." It occurs when a researcher rejects the null hypothesis ($H_0$) when it is actually true.

For this problem, the null hypothesis ($H_0$) is that there is no difference in the mean completion times between the two groups:

$$H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$$

highlights.text: rejecting the null hypothesis H0 when it is actually true

steps.title: Explain in context
steps.content:
Using the **Narrative Contextual Approach**, we focus on the researcher's specific mistake. 

A Type I error occurs if the researcher finds a significant difference in the puzzle completion times between Group X and Group Y when, in reality, no such difference exists. 

This means the researcher would conclude that one group is faster or slower than the other, even though the population means are actually equal.

highlights.text: concluding that there is a difference in the mean completion times between Group X and Group Y when the means are actually equal

- Conclude that there is insufficient evidence to suggest a difference in mean completion times between Group X and Group Y at the $1\%$ significance level <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the values
steps.content:
To draw a conclusion for our hypothesis test, we first need to identify the significance level ($\alpha$) and the $p$-value provided in the question.

*   The significance level is given as $1\%$, which we write as a decimal: $\alpha = 0.01$.
*   The $p$-value calculated for the test is $0.015$.

steps.title: Compare p-value to alpha
steps.content:
In statistics, the decision rule for a $p$-value comparison is:
*   If $p < \alpha$: Reject $H_0$ (significant result).
*   If $p \geq \alpha$: Do not reject $H_0$ (not a significant result).

Let's compare our values:
$$0.015 > 0.01$$

Since the $p$-value is greater than the significance level, we **do not reject** the null hypothesis ($H_0$).

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Finally, we must state our conclusion in the context of the math puzzle problem. 

Because we failed to reject $H_0$, we conclude that there is **insufficient evidence** at the $1\%$ significance level to suggest a difference in the mean completion times between Group X and Group Y.

**Marking Note:** To earn both marks, you must show the comparison ($0.015 > 0.01$) and then provide the conclusion specifically mentioning the "mean completion times."

*   Mean ($\mu_A$) = $12\text{ cm}$
*   Standard deviation ($\sigma_A$) = $1.5\text{ cm}$
*   Target height ($x$) = $13\text{ cm}$

highlights.text: mean height of 12 cm with a standard deviation of 1.5 cm

steps.title: Calculate the z-score
steps.content:
To use the standardization method, we need to find how many standard deviations the value $13\text{ cm}$ is from the mean. We do this by calculating the $z$-score using the formula:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} z &= \frac{13 - 12}{1.5} \\ z &= \frac{1}{1.5} \\ z &\approx 0.667 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the probability
steps.content:
We want the probability that a height is *greater than* $13\text{ cm}$, which corresponds to finding $P(Z > 0.667)$ on the standard normal distribution (where $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$).

Using a GDC or a standard normal table:

$$P(Z > 0.666\dots) \approx 0.252$$

gdc.instructions: In the Statistics/Distribution menu, select 'Normal Cdf'. Set the lower limit to 0.666667, upper limit to 9999, mean to 0, and standard deviation to 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select '2: normalcdf('. Enter the lower bound as 0.666667, the upper bound as a large number (like 1E99), μ as 0, and σ as 1.

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(0, 1).cdf(0.666667, infinity)' into an expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the area under the standard normal curve for Z > 0.666667.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, the probability is $0.252$. 

In an IB exam, you earn the first mark (**M1**) for correctly standardizing the value and the second mark (**A1**) for the final probability.

parts.content: Given that the $p$-value for the $t$-test is $0.085$, state the conclusion of the test and justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- Compare the $p$-value to the significance level: $0.085 < 0.10$ <marks>R1</marks>
- Conclude that since the $p$-value is less than the significance level, the null hypothesis is rejected and there is sufficient evidence to suggest that the mean height of seedlings in Greenhouse $A$ is greater than in Greenhouse $B$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the significance level
steps.content:
To conclude a statistical test, we first identify the significance level (often denoted as $\alpha$). In this problem, the test is performed at the $10\%$ significance level. 

To make a comparison easier, we convert this percentage into a decimal:

$$\alpha = 0.10$$

steps.title: Compare p-value to alpha
steps.content:
The **P-value Comparison** rule states:
- If $p < \alpha$, we **reject** the null hypothesis ($H_0$).
- If $p \geq \alpha$, we **fail to reject** the null hypothesis ($H_0$).

We are given a $p$-value of $0.085$. Let's compare it to our significance level:

$$0.085 < 0.10$$

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Since the $p$-value ($0.085$) is less than the significance level ($0.10$), we **reject the null hypothesis**. 

In the context of the problem, this means there is sufficient evidence at the $10\%$ level to suggest that the mean height of seedlings in Greenhouse $A$ is indeed greater than those in Greenhouse $B$.

In an IB exam, you earn marks by:
1. Showing the numerical comparison ($0.085 < 0.10$).
2. Stating the conclusion in the context of the seedling heights.

questions.specification:
A librarian at a university is investigating whether books are borrowed at the same rate across six different genres: Mystery, Sci-Fi, Biography, Romance, History, and Fantasy. The librarian proposes a model where the number of books borrowed is distributed equally among these six categories. To test this model, the librarian records the number of books borrowed from each genre over a specific period. The results are shown in the table below.

| Genre | Mystery | Sci-Fi | Biography | Romance | History | Fantasy |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

A chi-squared goodness of fit test at the 5% significance level is used to determine whether the librarian's model is appropriate. The critical value for this test is 11.07.

  - $H_1$: The ratio differs from the 1:2:1 distribution <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the expected frequencies:

  - Pink: $160 \times \frac{2}{4} = 80$
  - White: $160 \times \frac{1}{4} = 40$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the test statistic using the $\chi^2$ formula:
  - $\chi^2 = \frac{(35-40)^2}{40} + \frac{(88-80)^2}{80} + \frac{(37-40)^2}{40}$ <marks>M1</marks>

questions.specification:
A study examines whether the type of packaging (plastic, glass, or metal) affects the shelf life (short, medium, or long) of a product. Data from 180 products are recorded:

| | Short | Medium | Long |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Plastic** | 20 | 25 | 15 |
| **Glass** | 10 | 30 | 20 |
| **Metal** | 15 | 25 | 20 |

A $\chi^{2}$ test for independence is conducted at the $1\%$ significance level.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts ask for expected frequency in a contingency table and use of χ² test for independence, which involves constructing/using the table of expected frequencies.

parts.content: Calculate the expected frequency for products in glass packaging with a medium shelf life.
parts.markscheme:
Total products $= 180$
Total glass $= 10 + 30 + 20 = 60$
Total medium $= 25 + 30 + 25 = 80$ <marks>A1</marks>

Expected frequency $= \frac{60 \times 80}{180}$ 
$= 26.7$ (accept $26.666\dots$ or $\frac{80}{3}$) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the degrees of freedom and the critical value for this test.
parts.markscheme:
Degrees of freedom $= (3 - 1) \times (3 - 1) = 4$ <marks>A1</marks>

Critical value for $\chi^{2}$ with $4$ degrees of freedom at $1\%$ significance level is $13.277$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify rows and columns
steps.content:
To find the degrees of freedom ($df$) for a $\chi^{2}$ test for independence, we first need to count the number of rows ($r$) and columns ($c$) in our contingency table.

Looking at our table:
- **Rows (Packaging):** Plastic, Glass, Metal $\rightarrow r = 3$
- **Columns (Shelf life):** Short, Medium, Long $\rightarrow c = 3$

steps.title: Calculate degrees of freedom
steps.content:
The formula for degrees of freedom in a contingency table is:

$$df = (r - 1) \times (c - 1)$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} df &= (3 - 1) \times (3 - 1) \\ df &= 2 \times 2 \\ df &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the critical value
steps.content:
We are conducting the test at a $1\%$ significance level ($\alpha = 0.01$). 

The critical value is the point on the $\chi^{2}$ distribution with $4$ degrees of freedom where the area in the upper tail is $0.01$. This means the cumulative area (to the left) is $0.99$.

steps.title: Use the GDC function
steps.content: Using the **Inverse Chi-Squared** function on your calculator, you can find the critical value by inputting the cumulative area ($0.99$) and the degrees of freedom ($4$).

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the Statistics menu, then Distributions -> Chi-Squared -> Inverse. Input the Area as 0.99 and df as 4.

ti84.instructions: Go to [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR). Select 'invChi2('. Enter Area: 0.99 and df: 4. Select Paste and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: This function is not standard in the basic calculator, but on a GDC it returns approximately 13.277.

calculator.description: Calculate the critical value for Chi-Squared distribution

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
From the calculator, we get:

- **Degrees of freedom:** $4$
- **Critical value:** $13.277$

You would earn one mark for each of these correct values.

steps.title: Identify table dimensions
steps.content:
To find the degrees of freedom for a $\chi^{2}$ test for independence, we first need to count the number of rows ($r$) and columns ($c$) in our contingency table.

From the data provided:
- The rows represent the packaging types: **Plastic, Glass, Metal** ($r = 3$).
- The columns represent the shelf life: **Short, Medium, Long** ($c = 3$).

steps.title: Calculate degrees of freedom
steps.content:
The formula for degrees of freedom ($df$) in a contingency table is:

$$df = (r - 1) \times (c - 1)$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} df &= (3 - 1) \times (3 - 1) \\ df &= 2 \times 2 \\ df &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the subtraction and multiplication.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the result.

calculator.description: Verify the simple multiplication for degrees of freedom.

steps.title: Find the critical value
steps.content:
We are conducting the test at a **1% significance level** ($\alpha = 0.01$) with **4 degrees of freedom**. 

Using a standard chi-squared distribution table, we look for the row where $df = 4$ and the column for the $0.01$ significance level. 

The intersection of $df = 4$ and $\alpha = 0.01$ gives us the critical value:

$$\text{Critical Value} = 13.277$$

gdc.instructions: Use the inverse Chi-squared distribution function with area 0.99 and df 4.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'invChi2('. Enter Area: 0.99 (since 1% is in the upper tail), df: 4.

desmos.instructions: Inverse of the chi-squared distribution is not a direct standard function in basic Desmos, but you can use the distribution tool: 'chisquaredist(4).inverse_cdf(0.99)'.

calculator.description: Find the critical value using the inverse chi-squared function.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The degrees of freedom is $4$ and the critical value at the $1\%$ significance level is $13.277$.

parts.content: The $p$-value for this test is approximately $0.301$. State the conclusion of the test and justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
$0.301 > 0.01$ (or $p$-value $> 0.01$) <marks>R1</marks>

Fail to reject $H_0$. There is insufficient evidence to suggest that packaging type and shelf life are not independent (or: they are independent). <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Accept conclusion consistent with their comparison.

steps.title: Identify significance level
steps.content:
To make a decision in a $\chi^{2}$ test, we first need to express the significance level as a decimal. The problem states we are testing at the $1\%$ significance level, which we denote as $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = 0.01$$

steps.title: Compare p-value to alpha
steps.content:
Now, we compare the given $p$-value to our significance level $\alpha$. This is the standard "$p$-value comparison" approach used in **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability**.

We are given:
$$p \text{-value} = 0.301$$

Comparing the two values:
$$0.301 > 0.01$$

Since the $p$-value is greater than the significance level ($p > \alpha$), we have the first part of our justification.

gdc.instructions: On the main calculator screen, enter the comparison 0.301 > 0.01.

ti84.instructions: In the home screen, type 0.301 > 0.01. (The '>' symbol is found under [2nd] [MATH] for the TEST menu).

desmos.instructions: Type the inequality into a cell to check if it returns 'true'.

calculator.description: Compare 0.301 and 0.01 to verify the inequality.

highlights.text: p-value for this test is approximately 0.301

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Because the $p$-value is greater than $0.01$, we **fail to reject** the null hypothesis ($H_0$).

In the context of this problem, the null hypothesis for a $\chi^{2}$ test for independence is that the two variables are independent. Therefore, we conclude that there is **insufficient evidence** to suggest that packaging type and shelf life are related (they are independent).

**Awarding Marks:**
*   **1 mark** for the correct comparison ($0.301 > 0.01$).
*   **1 mark** for the correct conclusion in context (failing to reject $H_0$).

parts.content: Explain what a Type I error means in this context.
parts.markscheme: A Type I error is rejecting $H_0$ (concluding that packaging type and shelf life are not independent) when $H_0$ is actually true (they are independent). <marks>R1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall the general definition
steps.content:
In statistics, a **Type I error** occurs when we reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$) even though it is actually true. This is often called a "false positive."

To explain this in context, we first need to identify what the null hypothesis is for this specific study.

steps.title: Define the null hypothesis
steps.content:
For a $\chi^{2}$ test for independence, the null hypothesis ($H_0$) always assumes there is no relationship between the variables. 

In this case:
$$H_0: \text{Packaging type and shelf life are independent.}$$

steps.title: Apply to the context
steps.content:
Now, we combine the general definition with our specific variables. 

A Type I error means concluding that packaging type and shelf life are **not independent** (or that the packaging type *does* affect shelf life), when in reality they **are independent** (packaging type has *no effect* on shelf life).

steps.title: Final summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, you must mention both the decision made and the actual reality:

1.  **The Decision:** Concluding that packaging type and shelf life are not independent (rejecting $H_0$).
2.  **The Reality:** In fact, packaging type and shelf life are independent ($H_0$ is true).

questions.specification:
A survey of 150 students at a school records their preferred extracurricular activity (sports, arts, or music) and their year group (Year 10 or Year 11). The results are shown in the table below:

| | Sports | Arts | Music |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Year 10 | 30 | 15 | 20 |
| Year 11 | 25 | 40 | 20 |

A $\chi^{2}$ test is conducted at the $5 \%$ significance level to determine if the preferred activity is independent of the year group.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics and Probability > SL 4.11—Expected, observed, hypotheses, chi squared, gof, t-test > Question Type 3: Initiating the chi-squared independence test with the hypothesis and critical regions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Initiating the chi-squared independence test with the hypothesis and critical regions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is a chi-squared test of independence using a contingency table, including stating H0 and making a conclusion at a given significance level.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics and Probability > SL 4.11—Expected, observed, hypotheses, chi squared, gof, t-test > Question Type 4: Finding the table of expected frequencies for a chi-squared independence test
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Finding the table of expected frequencies for a chi-squared independence test
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for an expected frequency in a chi-squared independence test, which comes from constructing/using the expected-frequency table.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics and Probability > SL 4.11—Expected, observed, hypotheses, chi squared, gof, t-test > Question Type 2: Finding the p-value or critical value given the other for a specific distribution
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Finding the p-value or critical value given the other for a specific distribution
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) requires comparing the test statistic to a critical value or using a p-value to reach a conclusion.

parts.content: State the null hypothesis, $H_{0}$, for this test.
parts.markscheme:
- State the null hypothesis: The preferred extracurricular activity is independent of the year group. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<note>Accept "no association between activity and year group" or equivalent. Do not accept "not correlated" or "not related".</note>

steps.title: Identify the categorical variables
steps.content:
In a chi-squared ($\chi^2$) test for independence, we are looking at the relationship between two categorical variables. From the table provided, our two variables are:

1.  **Preferred extracurricular activity** (Sports, Arts, or Music)
2.  **Year group** (Year 10 or Year 11)

Before we can run the test, we must define our starting assumption, known as the null hypothesis.

highlights.text: preferred extracurricular activity (sports, arts, or music)

steps.title: Formulate the independence statement
steps.content:
The null hypothesis, $H_0$, always represents the status quo or the assumption that there is **no relationship** between the variables. 

For this specific test, we state that the two variables are independent of each other. This means that a student's year group does not affect their choice of extracurricular activity.

$$H_0: \text{The preferred extracurricular activity is independent of the year group.}$$

steps.title: Finalize the null hypothesis
steps.content:
When writing your answer for the IB, remember to use the word **independent** or the phrase **no association**. 

Avoid using terms like "correlation" or "related," as these are mathematically distinct from independence in the context of a $\chi^2$ test. 

**Correct statement:** The preferred extracurricular activity is independent of the year group.

highlights.text: The preferred extracurricular activity is independent of the year group.

parts.content: Calculate the expected frequency for Year 10 students choosing sports.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the total number of Year 10 students ($65$) and students choosing sports ($55$)
- Calculate the expected frequency: $\frac{65 \times 55}{150}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the final answer: $23.8$ (accept $23.833\dots$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the target cell
steps.content:
To calculate the expected frequency for **Year 10 students choosing sports**, we need to focus on that specific cell in our contingency table. 

We will need three values to use the expected frequency formula:
1. The total number of Year 10 students (the **row total**).
2. The total number of students who chose sports (the **column total**).
3. The total number of students in the survey (the **grand total**).

steps.title: Calculate the marginal totals
steps.content:
First, let's find the totals for the specific row and column involved:

**Row Total (Year 10):**
$$30 + 15 + 20 = 65$$

**Column Total (Sports):**
$$30 + 25 = 55$$

We already know the grand total is $150$.

steps.title: Apply the expected frequency formula
steps.content:
The expected frequency for any cell in a $\chi^2$ test of independence is calculated using the following fundamental formula:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = \frac{\text{Row Total} \times \text{Column Total}}{\text{Grand Total}}$$

This formula represents what the counts would be if the two variables (Year Group and Activity) were completely independent of each other.

steps.title: Perform the final calculation
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our values into the formula:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = \frac{65 \times 55}{150}$$

Calculating this gives:
$$\frac{3575}{150} = 23.8333...$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures (the standard for IB), we get **23.8**.

steps.content:
To find the expected frequency using a graphing calculator, we first need to identify the observed values from the contingency table provided in the question. We have two rows (Year 10 and Year 11) and three columns (Sports, Arts, and Music).

$$\begin{pmatrix} 30 & 15 & 20 \\ 25 & 40 & 20 \end{pmatrix}$$

steps.content:

We are looking for **Year 10 students choosing sports**, which corresponds to the first row and first column of the matrix ($E_{1,1}$).

According to the calculator results:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = \frac{65 \times 55}{150} \approx 23.8333...$$

parts.content: Find the degrees of freedom for this test.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the degrees of freedom: $(3-1) \times (2-1) = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content: Given the $\chi^{2}$ test statistic is $9.32$, determine the conclusion of the test. Justify your answer using the critical value or p-value.
parts.markscheme:
- Compare the test statistic to the critical value: $9.32 > 5.991$ (or p-value $< 0.05$) <marks>R1</marks>
- State the decision: Reject the null hypothesis, $H_{0}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State the final conclusion in context: There is sufficient evidence at the $5\%$ significance level to suggest that the preferred activity is not independent of the year group. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept a conclusion based on the p-value being less than $0.05$.</note>

steps.title: State the hypotheses
steps.content:
To start any hypothesis test, we must clearly define our Null Hypothesis ($H_0$) and Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$). For a $\chi^2$ test of independence, these are:

*   $H_0$: The preferred extracurricular activity is **independent** of the year group.
*   $H_1$: The preferred extracurricular activity is **not independent** of the year group.

We are testing this at the **5% significance level**.

steps.title: Calculate degrees of freedom
steps.content:
To find the critical value, we first need the degrees of freedom ($df$). For a contingency table, the formula is:

$$df = (r - 1)(c - 1)$$

Where $r$ is the number of rows and $c$ is the number of columns. Looking at our table, we have 2 rows (Year 10 and 11) and 3 columns (Sports, Arts, and Music):

$$\begin{aligned} df &= (2 - 1) \times (3 - 1) \\ df &= 1 \times 2 \\ df &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (2-1)*(3-1).

ti84.instructions: Simply multiply the dimensions minus one: (2-1)*(3-1).

desmos.instructions: Enter (2-1)*(3-1) to find the degrees of freedom.

calculator.description: Calculate degrees of freedom for a 2x3 contingency table.

steps.title: Find the critical value
steps.content:
Now we find the critical value for a $\chi^2$ distribution with $df = 2$ at the $0.05$ (5%) significance level. 

Using a GDC or a statistical table, the critical value is found to be:

$$\chi^2_{crit} = 5.991$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Inverse Chi-Square function with an area of 0.95 and degrees of freedom of 2.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'invChi2'. Enter Area: 0.95 (since we want the upper 5% tail) and df: 2.

desmos.instructions: Desmos does not have a direct inverse Chi-Square function; usually, this value is looked up in a table or found using the distribution functions.

calculator.description: Find the critical value for Chi-Square with df=2 and alpha=0.05.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Finally, we compare our given test statistic to the critical value:

*   Test Statistic: $9.32$
*   Critical Value: $5.991$

Since $9.32 > 5.991$, the test statistic falls in the **rejection region**.

ti84.instructions: Check if 9.32 is greater than 5.991.

desmos.instructions: Enter 9.32 > 5.991 to see if it returns 'true'.

calculator.description: Compare the test statistic to the critical value.

steps.title: State final conclusion
steps.content:
Because we rejected the null hypothesis, we state our conclusion in the context of the problem:

**Decision:** Reject $H_0$.

**Conclusion:** There is sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to suggest that the preferred extracurricular activity is **not independent** of the year group (i.e., there is an association between them).

steps.title: Calculate degrees of freedom
steps.content:
To find the $p$-value, we first need to determine the degrees of freedom ($df$) for the $\chi^2$ test of independence. The formula for $df$ is:

$$df = (r - 1)(c - 1)$$

where $r$ is the number of rows and $c$ is the number of columns in the contingency table. Looking at our table (Year 10 vs Year 11 and three activities):

$$\begin{aligned} r &= 2 \text{ (Year groups)} \\ c &= 3 \text{ (Activities)} \\ df &= (2 - 1)(3 - 1) = 1 \times 2 = 2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the p-value
steps.content:
Using a graphing display calculator (GDC), we calculate the $p$-value based on the given $\chi^2$ test statistic of $9.32$ and $2$ degrees of freedom. 

The $p$-value represents the probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as $9.32$, assuming the null hypothesis is true.

$$p\text{-value} = P(\chi^2 > 9.32) \approx 0.00947$$

gdc.instructions: In the Probability/Distribution menu, select 'Chi-squared CDF'. Enter 9.32 as the lower bound, a very large number (like 9999) as the upper bound, and 2 as the degrees of freedom.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'χ²cdf(', and enter: lower: 9.32, upper: 1E99, df: 2.

desmos.instructions: Use the Chi-Squared distribution function and find the area from 9.32 to infinity.

calculator.description: Calculate the p-value for a Chi-squared distribution with df=2 and test statistic 9.32.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Now we compare our calculated $p$-value to the given significance level ($\alpha = 0.05$).

$$\begin{aligned} p\text{-value} &\approx 0.00947 \\ 0.00947 &< 0.05 \end{aligned}$$

Since the $p$-value is less than the significance level, we **reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$)**. 

**Conclusion in context:** There is sufficient evidence at the $5\%$ significance level to suggest that the preferred extracurricular activity is **not independent** of the year group.

parts.content: State the null and alternative hypotheses for this test.
parts.markscheme:
$H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$
$H_1: \mu_X > \mu_Y$
<marks>M1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the population parameters
steps.content:
To set up our hypotheses, we first need to identify the population parameters we are testing. Here, we are comparing the average (mean) completion times for two groups: Class X and Class Y.

We use the Greek letter $\mu$ (mu) to represent the population mean:
- $\mu_X$: the mean time taken by Class X.
- $\mu_Y$: the mean time taken by Class Y.

steps.title: State the null hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis, $H_0$, is the statement of "no effect" or "no difference." It assumes that any difference observed in our sample data is just due to chance.

In this case, the null hypothesis states that the average time for Class X is equal to the average time for Class Y:

$$H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$$

steps.title: State the alternative hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis, $H_1$, represents the claim we are trying to find evidence for. The question asks if Class X takes **longer than** Class Y on average.

"Longer than" implies that the mean for Class X is greater than the mean for Class Y. This gives us a one-tailed test:

$$H_1: \mu_X > \mu_Y$$

steps.title: Summary of hypotheses
steps.content:
Combining our steps, the final answer required for the markscheme is:

$$\begin{aligned} H_0: \mu_X &= \mu_Y \\ H_1: \mu_X &> \mu_Y \end{aligned}$$

This follows the standard IB format for a two-sample t-test as covered in **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability**.

steps.title: Identify the claim
steps.content: To set up our hypotheses, we first look at the teacher's goal. They want to determine if Class X takes **longer on average** than Class Y to complete the coding task. This tells us we are comparing two population means, $\mu_X$ and $\mu_Y$.

steps.title: State the null hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis ($H_0$) is the "baseline" assumption that there is no difference between the two groups. 

In this case, the **null hypothesis** states that the mean time taken by Class X is equal to the mean time taken by Class Y. 

$$H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$$

steps.title: State the alternative hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis ($H_1$) reflects the specific claim we are testing. Since we are checking if Class X takes *longer* than Class Y, this is a one-tailed test.

The **alternative hypothesis** states that the mean time taken by Class X is greater than the mean time taken by Class Y.

$$H_1: \mu_X > \mu_Y$$

steps.title: Finalize the hypotheses
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you can state these using symbols or words, but it is best to provide both for clarity:

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** The population mean time for Class X is equal to the population mean time for Class Y.
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$):** The population mean time for Class X is greater than the population mean time for Class Y.

steps.title: Define the population parameters
steps.content:
In a statistical test, we first define the parameters we are comparing. Let:
- $\mu_X$ be the population mean time taken by Class X.
- $\mu_Y$ be the population mean time taken by Class Y.

steps.title: State the Null Hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis ($H_0$) is the statement of "no effect" or "no difference." Using the **Difference of Means** approach, we assume the difference between the two population means is zero.

$$H_0: \mu_X - \mu_Y = 0$$

steps.title: State the Alternative Hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis ($H_1$) represents the claim we are testing. The question asks if Class X **takes longer on average** than Class Y, which means $\mu_X > \mu_Y$.

Subtracting $\mu_Y$ from both sides gives us the difference form:

$$H_1: \mu_X - \mu_Y > 0$$

steps.title: Verify with GDC logic
steps.content: When you eventually perform this test on your calculator, selecting the correct "tail" is crucial. Since our alternative hypothesis is $\mu_X > \mu_Y$ (or $\mu_X - \mu_Y > 0$), this is a **one-tailed test** where we look for values in the right tail of the distribution.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select [TEST], then [T], then [2-SAMPLE]. Set the alternative hypothesis (Alternative) to [μ1 > μ2].

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT], arrow to [TESTS], and select [4: 2-SampTTest]. For the '$μ1$' input, select [> μ2] to correspond with our $H_1: μ_X > μ_Y$.

desmos.instructions: Desmos does not have a built-in 2-sample t-test menu like handheld GDCs, but you would use the 'ttest' function or visualize the distribution to see if the difference $45 - 42 = 3$ falls in the critical region defined by the $> 0$ hypothesis.

parts.content: Explain what a Type II error means in this context.
parts.markscheme:
A Type II error is failing to reject $H_0$ (concluding that the mean times are not significantly different) when $H_0$ is false (Class X actually takes longer on average than Class Y).
<marks>A1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Given the t-test statistic is $2.10$ with $53$ degrees of freedom, find the $p$-value and state the conclusion of the test.
parts.markscheme:
For $t = 2.10$, $df = 53$, one-tailed $p$-value $\approx 0.020$ 
<marks>A1</marks>

Since $0.020 > 0.01$, do not reject $H_0$ (or fail to reject $H_0$).
<marks>A1</marks>

There is insufficient evidence to suggest that Class X takes longer on average than Class Y at the $1\%$ significance level.
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the $p$-value
steps.content:
In a one-tailed t-test where we are checking if Class X takes **longer** than Class Y, the $p$-value represents the probability that the $t$-statistic is greater than our calculated value. 

We are looking for:

$$p\text{-value} = P(T > 2.10) \text{ where } df = 53$$

steps.title: Calculate using GDC
steps.content:
To find this probability, we use the cumulative distribution function for the $t$-distribution ($tcdf$) on your graphing calculator. Since we want the area to the *right* of $2.10$, we set our lower bound to $2.10$ and our upper bound to a very large number (like $10^{99}$).

Using the calculator, we find:

$$p\text{-value} \approx 0.0200$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Statistics menu.
2. Select DIST -> STUDENT-T -> tcd.
3. Enter:
   - Lower: 2.10
   - Upper: 9.99E99
   - df: 53
4. Execute to find the probability.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR).
2. Select 6: tcdf(.
3. Enter: 
   - lower: 2.10
   - upper: 1E99
   - df: 53
4. Select Paste and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type 'tdist(53)' to define the distribution.
2. Click 'Cumulative Probability (CDF)'.
3. Enter 2.10 as the 'Min' and leave 'Max' as infinity.

calculator.description: Calculate the p-value using the t-distribution cumulative function.

steps.title: Compare and Conclude
steps.content:
Now we compare our $p$-value to the significance level $\alpha = 0.01$ (which is $1\%$). 

Since $0.0200 > 0.01$, the $p$-value is greater than the significance level. This means we **do not reject** the null hypothesis $H_0$. 

**Conclusion:** At the $1\%$ significance level, there is **insufficient evidence** to suggest that Class X takes longer on average than Class Y.

steps.title: Define the test type
steps.content:
To determine if Class X takes longer on average than Class Y, we use a one-tailed test. The hypotheses are:

- $H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$ (The average times are the same)
- $H_1: \mu_X > \mu_Y$ (Class X takes longer on average)

We are testing at the $1\%$ significance level, which means our threshold for rejection is $\alpha = 0.01$.

highlights.text: determine if Class X takes longer on average than Class Y

steps.title: Calculate the p-value
steps.content:
Using a Graphing Display Calculator (GDC), we can find the $p$-value corresponding to the given test statistic $t = 2.10$ and $df = 53$. For a one-tailed test (right-tailed), the $p$-value is the probability $P(T > 2.10)$.

From the GDC:

$$p\text{-value} \approx 0.0203$$

steps.content: We now compare the calculated $p$-value to the significance level $\alpha = 0.01$. Since $0.0203 > 0.01$, the $p$-value is greater than the significance level, and we **do not reject** the null hypothesis $H_0$.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content: Since we failed to reject $H_0$, there is **insufficient evidence** at the $1\%$ significance level to suggest that Class X takes longer on average than Class Y to complete the coding task.

parts.content: State the null and alternative hypotheses for the $\chi^{2}$ test.
parts.markscheme:
$H_{0}$: Faculty and preferred learning resource are independent. <marks>A1</marks>

$H_{1}$: Faculty and preferred learning resource are not independent. <marks>A1</marks>

Note: Accept "no association" for $H_{0}$ and "association exists" for $H_{1}$.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.content:
To state the hypotheses for a $\chi^{2}$ test, we first identify the two categorical variables being investigated. From the question, these are:
1. **Faculty** (Science, Arts, or Business)
2. **Preferred learning resource** (Textbooks, Videos, or Lectures)

highlights.text: investigate whether their faculty and preferred learning resource (textbooks, online videos, lectures) are independent

steps.title: State the null hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis ($H_{0}$) always assumes that there is no relationship between the variables. Using the **Independence-Based Phrasing** approach, we state that the variables are independent of each other.

$$H_{0}: \text{Faculty and preferred learning resource are independent.}$$

steps.title: State the alternative hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis ($H_{1}$) is the logical opposite of the null hypothesis. It suggests that a relationship exists between the variables.

$$H_{1}: \text{Faculty and preferred learning resource are not independent.}$$

steps.title: Review marksheet requirements
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for correctly stating $H_{0}$ and **1 mark** for correctly stating $H_{1}$. 

Remember: For a test of independence, always start by assuming they are independent ($H_{0}$) and then suggest they are not independent ($H_{1}$).

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.content:
To begin a $\chi^{2}$ test for independence, we first need to identify the two categorical variables we are comparing. In this study, we are looking at:
1. The **Faculty** (Science, Arts, or Business)
2. The **Preferred learning resource** (Textbooks, Videos, or Lectures)

The goal of our hypotheses is to describe whether or not these two variables are related.

highlights.text: faculty and preferred learning resource (textbooks, online videos, lectures) are independent

steps.title: Define the null hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis, $H_{0}$, represents the status quo—the idea that there is no relationship between the variables. Using the **association-based phrasing** requested, we state that the choice of resource does not depend on the faculty.

$$H_{0}: \text{There is no association between faculty and preferred learning resource.}$$

highlights.text: $H_{0}$: Faculty and preferred learning resource are independent.

steps.title: Define the alternative hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis, $H_{1}$, is the opposite of $H_{0}$. It claims that there is indeed a relationship or a pattern between the two variables. Using the **association-based phrasing**, we state:

$$H_{1}: \text{An association exists between faculty and preferred learning resource.}$$

Note: In the IB markscheme, using "no association" and "association exists" is a perfectly valid way to earn both marks for this part.

highlights.text: $H_{1}$: Faculty and preferred learning resource are not independent.

parts.content: Calculate the expected frequency for Science students preferring textbooks.
parts.markscheme:
Total students $= 200$, total Science $= 80$, total textbooks $= 60$.
<marks>A1</marks>

Expected frequency $= \frac{80}{200} \times \frac{60}{200} \times 200 = \frac{80 \times 60}{200} = 24$.
<marks>M1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant totals
steps.content:
To calculate the expected frequency for a specific cell in a contingency table, we first need to identify three key values from the data provided: the total for that row, the total for that column, and the grand total of all students surveyed.

From the table, we look at:
- The **Science** row total: $80$
- The **Textbooks** column total: $60$
- The **Grand Total**: $200$

steps.title: Apply expected frequency formula
steps.content:
The expected frequency ($f_e$) for any cell under the assumption of independence is calculated using the following formula:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = \frac{\text{row total} \times \text{column total}}{\text{grand total}}$$

This formula represents the frequency we would expect to see if there were no relationship between faculty and resource preference. This concept is covered in **Topic 4.11: Chi-squared tests**.

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our identified values into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} f_e &= \frac{80 \times 60}{200} \\ f_e &= \frac{4800}{200} \\ f_e &= 24 \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, identifying the totals earns you the first mark, and correctly applying the calculation earns the remaining marks.

steps.title: Identify the key totals
steps.content:
To calculate the expected frequency using a probabilistic approach, we first need to identify the total number of students in the relevant categories from the table:

*   **Total Science students**: 80
*   **Total students preferring Textbooks**: 60
*   **Grand total of students**: 200

These values are the foundation for our probability calculations.

steps.title: Calculate independent probabilities
steps.content:
Under the null hypothesis for a $\chi^{2}$ test, we assume the two variables (Faculty and Resource) are independent. If they are independent, the probability of a student being in Science **and** preferring Textbooks is the product of their individual probabilities.

First, find the probability of being in the Science faculty:

$$P(\text{Science}) = \frac{80}{200} = 0.4$$

Next, find the probability of preferring Textbooks:

$$P(\text{Textbooks}) = \frac{60}{200} = 0.3$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the row total by the grand total, then the column total by the grand total.

ti84.instructions: Enter 80 / 200 to find P(Science). Enter 60 / 200 to find P(Textbooks).

desmos.instructions: Divide 80 by 200 and 60 by 200.

calculator.description: Calculate individual probabilities for faculty and resource preference.

steps.title: Calculate expected frequency
steps.content:
The expected frequency ($f_e$) is found by multiplying the joint probability of both events occurring (assuming independence) by the total number of students ($N = 200$).

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = P(\text{Science}) \times P(\text{Textbooks}) \times N$$

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = 0.4 \times 0.3 \times 200$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the calculated probabilities (0.4 and 0.3) by the total population (200).

ti84.instructions: Multiply 0.4 by 0.3, then multiply the result by 200.

calculator.description: Multiply the probabilities by the total sample size to find the expected frequency.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
Evaluating the expression gives us the final expected frequency:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = 24$$

In an IB exam, you can also write this in one step as:

$$\frac{80 \times 60}{200} = 24$$

This calculation demonstrates that if Faculty and Resource preference were perfectly independent, we would expect 24 Science students to prefer textbooks.

steps.title: Identify the relevant totals
steps.content: To find the expected frequency, we first need to identify the three key values from the table: the total number of students in the Science faculty, the total number of students who prefer textbooks, and the grand total of all students surveyed.

steps.title: Calculate Science student percentage
steps.content:
Using the **Proportional Reasoning** approach, we find what percentage of the total student body belongs to the Science faculty.

$$\text{Percentage of Science students} = \frac{\text{Total Science}}{\text{Grand Total}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Percentage} = \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%$$

If the faculty and learning resource are independent, we expect that same percentage ($40\%$) of the textbook-preferring group to be Science students. 

We apply this percentage to the total number of students who prefer textbooks ($60$):

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = 40\% \text{ of } 60$$

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = 0.40 \times 60$$
