parts.content: For Science students, find the probability that two randomly selected students both prefer lectures, assuming sampling with replacement.
parts.markscheme:
Science students preferring lectures $= 30$.
<marks>A1</marks>

$P(\text{both lectures}) = \frac{30}{80} \times \frac{30}{80} = \frac{9}{64} \approx 0.141$.
<marks>M1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify Science faculty data
steps.content:
To find the probability for Science students, we first look at the row labeled **Science** in the table. 

We need the number of Science students who prefer lectures and the total number of students in that faculty:
- Science students preferring lectures: $30$
- Total Science students: $80$

steps.title: Calculate single student probability
steps.content:
The probability that one randomly selected Science student prefers lectures is the number of Science students preferring lectures divided by the total number of Science students.

$$P(\text{Lectures}) = \frac{30}{80} = \frac{3}{8}$$

steps.title: Apply Direct Probability Multiplication
steps.content:
Since the question specifies **sampling with replacement**, the selection of the second student is independent of the first. To find the probability that both students prefer lectures, we multiply the individual probabilities together:

$$P(\text{both prefer lectures}) = \frac{30}{80} \times \frac{30}{80}$$

steps.title: Determine final result
steps.content:
Using the Direct Probability Multiplication approach, we calculate the final value. You can provide the answer as an exact fraction or a decimal rounded to three significant figures.

$$\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{3}{8} = \frac{9}{64} \approx 0.141$$

2. Press EXE.

2. Press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the squared probability and convert to decimal.

steps.title: Identify parameters for Binomial
steps.content:
To find the probability that both students prefer lectures, we can model this as a Binomial experiment. Since we are sampling **with replacement**, the selection of each student is independent, and the probability of "success" (preferring lectures) stays the same for both selections.

First, let's find the total number of Science students and how many prefer lectures from the table:
- Total Science students: $80$
- Science students preferring lectures: $30$

The probability ($p$) of one student preferring lectures is:
$$p = \frac{30}{80} = 0.375$$

Since we are selecting **two** students, our number of trials is $n = 2$.

steps.title: Define the Binomial model
steps.content:
Let $X$ be the number of students who prefer lectures. We can say $X$ follows a Binomial distribution:

$$X \sim B(n, p)$$

$$X \sim B(2, 0.375)$$

We want to find the probability that **exactly 2** students prefer lectures, which is $P(X = 2)$.

steps.title: Calculate using the formula
steps.content:
Using the Binomial probability formula from the data booklet:

$$P(X = r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r (1-p)^{n-r}$$

Substitute our values ($n=2, r=2, p=0.375$):

$$\begin{aligned} P(X = 2) &= \binom{2}{2} (0.375)^2 (1-0.375)^{2-2} \\ &= 1 \times (0.375)^2 \times 1 \\ &= (0.375)^2 \end{aligned}$$

This simplifies to the same logic as the multiplication rule: $P(\text{First prefers lectures}) \times P(\text{Second prefers lectures})$.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Probability menu, select Distributions, then Binomial PDF. Enter trials: 2, prob: 0.375, and x: 2.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'binompdf(', enter 'n: 2', 'p: 30/80', 'x value: 2', and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type the binomial distribution function and use the PDF at x=2.

calculator.description: Calculate the final probability

steps.title: State the final probability
steps.content:
Calculating the final value:

$$P(\text{both prefer lectures}) = \left(\frac{30}{80}\right)^2 = \frac{900}{6400} = \frac{9}{64}$$

Converting to a decimal (rounded to 3 significant figures):
$$P(\text{both prefer lectures}) \approx 0.141$$

parts.content: Explain what a Type I error means in this context and discuss its potential impact on the university's resource allocation decisions.
parts.markscheme:
Type I error: Rejecting $H_{0}$ (concluding dependence) when $H_{0}$ is true (faculty and resource preference are actually independent).
<marks>A1</marks>

Impact: The university may misallocate resources by faculty (e.g., over-investing in specific resources for one faculty) based on a false assumption of dependence, leading to inefficient budgeting.
<marks>A1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the Null Hypothesis
steps.content:
To understand a Type I error, we first need to clearly state our null hypothesis ($H_{0}$). In a $\chi^{2}$ test for independence, the null hypothesis always assumes that there is no relationship between the two variables.

In this context, the null hypothesis is:

$$H_{0}: \text{Faculty and preferred learning resource are independent.}$$

highlights.text: faculty and preferred learning resource (textbooks, online videos, lectures) are independent

steps.title: Define Type I Error
steps.content:
A **Type I error** occurs when we reject the null hypothesis ($H_{0}$) even though it is actually true. 

In simpler terms, it is a "false positive": we conclude that a relationship exists when, in reality, it does not. This is directly related to the significance level ($\alpha$) of the test.

steps.title: Contextualize the Error
steps.content:
Applying this definition to our university survey:

A Type I error means the university concludes that a student's faculty **does** influence their preferred learning resource, when in fact students across all faculties have the same general preferences (they are independent).

steps.title: Evaluate the Impact
steps.content:
If the university makes this error, they will act on a false assumption of dependence. This leads to **unnecessary faculty-specific resource strategies**.

For example, the university might spend a large portion of the Science faculty budget on online videos because they believe Science students prefer them more than others. If the variables are actually independent, this is an **inefficient use of the budget** and a misallocation of resources, as the specialized investment wasn't actually needed.

highlights.text: The university may misallocate resources by faculty (e.g., over-investing in specific resources for one faculty) based on a false assumption of dependence, leading to inefficient budgeting.

parts.content: Test at the 1% significance level whether the die is fair. State your hypotheses clearly and show all working.
parts.markscheme:
- Clearly stated hypotheses:
  $H_0$: The die is fair (all probabilities = $1/6$)
  $H_1$: The die is not fair (at least one probability $\neq 1/6$) <marks>B1</marks>

- Expected frequency calculation: $E = 120/6 = 20$ for each number <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct formula used: $\chi^2 = \sum\frac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ <marks>B1</marks>

- Calculation:
  $\frac{(15-20)^2}{20} + \frac{(22-20)^2}{20} + \frac{(18-20)^2}{20} + \frac{(25-20)^2}{20} + \frac{(19-20)^2}{20} + \frac{(21-20)^2}{20}$
  $= 1.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 1.25 + 0.05 + 0.05 = 3.00$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Degrees of freedom = $6 - 1 = 5$ <marks>B1</marks>

- Critical value at 1% = 15.086 <marks>B1</marks>

- Since $3.00 < 15.086$, fail to reject $H_0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Conclusion: Insufficient evidence to suggest the die is unfair <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification:
On $90$ journeys to his office, Isaac noted whether or not it rained. He also recorded his journey time to the office, and classified each journey as short, medium or long.

Of the $90$ journeys to the office, there were $3$ short journeys when it rained, $22$ medium journeys when it rained, and $15$ long journeys when it rained. There were also $14$ short journeys when it did not rain.

Isaac carried out a $\chi^2$ test at the $5\%$ level of significance on these data, looking at the weather and the types of journeys.

steps.title: Identify the two variables
steps.content:
To write a null hypothesis for a $\chi^2$ test of independence, we first need to identify the two categorical variables being studied. Based on the question, Isaac is looking at:
1. The **weather** (whether it rained or not).
2. The **type of journey** (short, medium, or long).

highlights.text: weather and the types of journeys

steps.title: State the null hypothesis
steps.content:
In a $\chi^2$ test of independence, the null hypothesis ($H_0$) always assumes that there is **no relationship** between the two variables. 

We state this by asserting that the variables are **independent**. 

$$H_0: \text{The type of journey and whether it rained are independent}$$

highlights.text: type of journey and whether it rained are independent

steps.title: Verify against requirements
steps.content:
In the IB exam, you can also use the phrase "no association." However, avoid terms like "not related" or "not correlated," as these are not mathematically precise for this specific test.

Final Answer:
$$H_0: \text{The type of journey and whether it rained are independent}$$

parts.content: Find the expected number of short trips when it rained.
parts.markscheme:

- Substitute into the expected frequency formula: $\frac{17 \times 40}{90}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the expected value: $7.56$ ($7.55555\dots$, $\frac{68}{9}$) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the required totals
steps.content:
To find the expected frequency for a specific cell in a $\chi^2$ contingency table, we need to know three things:
1. The **row total** (total number of short journeys).
2. The **column total** (total number of journeys when it rained).
3. The **grand total** (total number of journeys Isaac recorded).

This calculation is part of **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability**. If you need a refresher on how contingency tables work, it's worth reviewing that section!

highlights.text: Find the expected number of short trips when it rained.

steps.title: Calculate the marginal totals
steps.content:
First, we sum the values provided in the question to find our marginal totals.

Total number of journeys when it **rained**:
$$3 \text{ (short)} + 22 \text{ (medium)} + 15 \text{ (long)} = 40$$

Total number of **short** journeys:
$$3 \text{ (rained)} + 14 \text{ (did not rain)} = 17$$

The **grand total** is already given as $90$.

gdc.instructions: Input 3 + 22 + 15 and 3 + 14.

ti84.instructions: Type 3 + 22 + 15 then press ENTER. Type 3 + 14 then press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter the sums directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Summing the values for the totals.

highlights.text: 14 short journeys when it did not rain

steps.title: Apply expected frequency formula
steps.content:
Now we use the expected frequency formula. This is a fundamental concept for $\chi^2$ independence tests:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = \frac{\text{Row Total} \times \text{Column Total}}{\text{Grand Total}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = \frac{17 \times 40}{90}$$

steps.title: Calculate final value
steps.content:
Perform the final multiplication and division:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected Value} &= \frac{680}{90} \\
&= \frac{68}{9} \\
&\approx 7.555\dots
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get $7.56$.

ti84.instructions: Press (17 * 40) / 90 then ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Input the fraction 680/90.

calculator.description: Calculating the final expected frequency.

steps.title: Understand the probability approach
steps.content:
To find the **expected number** of short trips when it rained using the probability approach, we assume that the two variables (journey type and weather) are independent. 

If they are independent, the expected frequency is calculated by finding the probability of a journey being 'short', multiplying it by the probability of it 'raining', and then multiplying that result by the total number of journeys ($N$).

$$E = P(\text{Short}) \times P( \text{Rain}) \times N$$

steps.title: Calculate total short journeys
steps.content:
First, we need to find the total number of short journeys recorded. We look at the data for both when it rained and when it didn't rain:

$$\text{Total Short} = 3 \text{ (rained)} + 14 \text{ (did not rain)} = 17$$

So, the probability of a journey being short is:
$$P(\text{Short}) = \frac{17}{90}$$

highlights.text: 14 short journeys when it did not rain

steps.title: Calculate total rainy journeys
steps.content:
Next, we find the total number of journeys where it rained across all journey types:

$$\text{Total Rain} = 3 \text{ (short)} + 22 \text{ (medium)} + 15 \text{ (long)} = 40$$

So, the probability of it raining is:
$$P(\text{Rain}) = \frac{40}{90}$$

steps.title: Calculate the expected value
steps.content:
Now, we multiply the two independent probabilities by the total sample size ($90$):

$$\text{Expected Value} = \frac{17}{90} \times \frac{40}{90} \times 90$$

This simplifies to the standard $\chi^2$ expected frequency formula:

$$\text{Expected Value} = \frac{17 \times 40}{90} = \frac{680}{90}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division to find the decimal value.

ti84.instructions: Enter the fraction in the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Divide 680 by 90 to get the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the final expected value.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Evaluating the fraction gives us the expected number of short trips when it rained.

$$\text{Expected Value} \approx 7.56$$ 

(Note: In the IB, you can leave this as a fraction $\frac{68}{9}$ or round it to $3$ significant figures as $7.56$.)

methods.label: Comparison of p-value with Significance Level

steps.title: Identify the key values
steps.content:
To determine the conclusion of the $\chi^2$ test, we first need to identify the $p$-value and the significance level ($\alpha$) from the question.

From the problem, we have:
- $p$-value = $0.0206$
- Significance level = $5\% = 0.05$

steps.title: Compare p-value and alpha
steps.content:
In statistics, we compare the $p$-value to our significance level ($0.05$) to decide whether to reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$):

- If $p \leq 0.05$, we **reject** $H_0$.
- If $p > 0.05$, we **do not reject** $H_0$.

Let's check our values:
$$0.0206 < 0.05$$

desmos.instructions: Type the comparison into a cell to see the boolean result.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since $0.0206 < 0.05$, we **reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$)**.

In the context of the problem, this means there is sufficient evidence to suggest that the type of journey and whether it rained are **not independent**.

| Store $Z$ | 35 | 35 | 55 | 125 |
| **Total** | 100 | 100 | 100 | 300 |

A $\chi^2$ test for independence is conducted at the $5\%$ significance level.

The retailer also examines the relationship between service complaints and store satisfaction scores (out of 100) using Spearman's rank correlation coefficient.

parts.content: State the null and alternative hypotheses for the $\chi^2$ test.

$H_0$: Complaint type and store are independent. <marks>A1</marks>
$H_1$: Complaint type and store are not independent. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the categorical variables
steps.content:
To perform a $\chi^2$ test for independence, we first need to identify the two categorical variables being compared in the contingency table. 

Looking at the data provided, the variables are:
1. **Store** (with categories $X, Y,$ and $Z$)
2. **Complaint Type** (with categories Product Quality, Service, and Delivery)

The goal of this test is to determine if the type of complaint a customer makes depends on which store they visited.

steps.title: State the Null Hypothesis
steps.content:
The null hypothesis, $H_0$, always represents the 'status quo' or the assumption that there is **no relationship** between the variables. In a $\chi^2$ test of independence, we state that the variables are independent of each other.

$$H_0: \text{Store and Complaint Type are independent}$$

steps.title: State the Alternative Hypothesis
steps.content:
The alternative hypothesis, $H_1$, is the opposite of the null hypothesis. It suggests that there **is a relationship** or an association between the two variables.

$$H_1: \text{Store and Complaint Type are not independent}$$

(Note: You can also state this as "There is an association between Store and Complaint Type.")

steps.title: Review Markscheme Requirements
steps.content:
In the IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for correctly stating $H_0$ and **1 mark** for $H_1$. Ensure you use the specific names of the variables mentioned in the question to get full credit.

Final Answer:
- $H_0$: Complaint type and store are independent. 
- $H_1$: Complaint type and store are not independent.

parts.content: Calculate the expected frequency for Store $Y$ service complaints.
parts.markscheme:
Store $Y$ total $= 35 + 40 + 25 = 100$
Service total $= 25 + 40 + 35 = 100$
Grand total $= 300$ <marks>A1</marks>

Expected frequency $= \frac{100 \times 100}{300}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$= 33.3$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify row and column totals
steps.content:
To calculate the expected frequency for a specific cell in a contingency table, we first need to identify three values: the total for that row, the total for that column, and the grand total of the entire sample.

From the table provided in the question:
- The row total for **Store $Y$** is $100$.
- The column total for **Service** complaints is $100$.
- The **Grand Total** of transactions is $300$.

Identifying these three correct values earns you the first mark.

steps.title: Apply the expected frequency formula
steps.content:
In a $\chi^2$ test for independence, the expected frequency ($f_e$) is calculated using the following fundamental formula:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = \frac{\text{row total} \times \text{column total}}{\text{total number of observations}}$$

This formula represents what the frequency would be if the two variables (Store and Complaint Type) were completely independent of each other.

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Substitute our values into the formula to find the expected frequency for Store $Y$ service complaints:

$$\begin{aligned} f_e &= \frac{100 \times 100}{300} \\ f_e &= \frac{10000}{300} \\ f_e &= 33.333... \end{aligned}$$

In IB Mathematics, we typically round our final answers to **3 significant figures** unless specified otherwise.

desmos.instructions: Type (100 * 100) / 300 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the expected frequency using the row, column, and grand totals.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The expected frequency for Store $Y$ service complaints is:

$$\text{Expected frequency} = 33.3$$

According to the markscheme, you receive 1 mark for identifying the totals, 1 method mark for the correct substitution, and 1 accuracy mark for the final value.

steps.content:
To find the expected frequency for **Store $Y$ service complaints**, we first need to identify the observed values for that row and column from the table.

*   **Column (Service):** Total complaints $= 100$

By viewing Matrix $[B]$, the value in the second row (Store $Y$) and second column (Service) is:

Rounding to 3 significant figures, as is standard in IB Mathematics:

$$\text{Expected Frequency} = 33.3$$

parts.content: Compute the $\chi^2$ test statistic for this data.
parts.markscheme:
Expected frequencies ($E = \frac{\text{Row Total} \times \text{Col Total}}{\text{Grand Total}}$):
Store $X$: Quality = 25, Service = 25, Delivery = 25
Store $Y$: Quality = 33.3, Service = 33.3, Delivery = 33.3
Store $Z$: Quality = 41.7, Service = 41.7, Delivery = 41.7 <marks>A1</marks>

$\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\chi^2 = \left[ \frac{(30-25)^2}{25} + \frac{(25-25)^2}{25} + \frac{(20-25)^2}{25} \right] + \left[ \frac{(35-33.333)^2}{33.333} + \frac{(40-33.333)^2}{33.333} + \frac{(25-33.333)^2}{33.333} \right] + \left[ \frac{(35-41.667)^2}{41.667} + \frac{(35-41.667)^2}{41.667} + \frac{(55-41.667)^2}{41.667} \right]$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\chi^2 = [1 + 0 + 1] + [0.0833 + 1.333 + 2.083] + [1.067 + 1.067 + 4.267]$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\chi^2 \approx 11.9$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Hence, conclude the test at the $5\%$ significance level. The critical value for this test is $9.488$.
parts.markscheme:
Degrees of freedom $= (3-1)(3-1) = 4$ <marks>A1</marks>

Since $\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 11.9 > 9.488$ (critical value), we reject $H_0$. <marks>A1</marks>

There is sufficient evidence at the $5\%$ significance level to suggest that complaint type is not independent of the store. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate Degrees of Freedom
steps.content:
To conclude a $\chi^2$ test for independence, we first need to determine the **degrees of freedom** ($df$). This depends on the number of rows ($r$) and columns ($c$) in our contingency table.

The formula is:
$$df = (r - 1) \times (c - 1)$$

In this case, we have 3 stores (rows) and 3 complaint types (columns):
$$\begin{aligned} df &= (3 - 1) \times (3 - 1) \\ df &= 2 \times 2 \\ df &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (3-1)*(3-1) on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter (3-1)*(3-1) on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type (3-1)*(3-1) into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the degrees of freedom.

steps.title: Compare Chi-Squared to Critical Value
steps.content:
Now we use the **Critical Value Comparison** approach. We compare our calculated $\chi^2$ statistic ($11.9$) to the critical value provided in the question.

$$\begin{aligned} \chi^2_{\text{calc}} &= 11.9 \\ \text{Critical Value} &= 9.488 \end{aligned}$$

Since $11.9 > 9.488$, our calculated value falls into the **rejection region** (the tail of the distribution).

highlights.text: critical value for this test is 9.488

steps.title: State the Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Because the calculated statistic exceeds the critical value, we **reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$)**.

In the context of the problem, this means there is sufficient evidence at the $5\%$ significance level to suggest that the **type of complaint is not independent of the store**. In other words, there is a relationship between which store a customer visits and the kind of complaint they are likely to make.

steps.title: State the hypotheses
steps.content:
To start any $\chi^2$ test for independence, we first define our null hypothesis ($H_0$) and alternative hypothesis ($H_1$):

*   $H_0$: Complaint type is independent of the store.
*   $H_1$: Complaint type is not independent of the store.

steps.title: Determine degrees of freedom
steps.content:
Before using the calculator, we need the degrees of freedom ($df$). For a contingency table, the formula is:

$$df = (\text{number of rows} - 1) \times (\text{number of columns} - 1)$$

In our case, we have 3 stores (rows) and 3 complaint types (columns):

$$\begin{aligned} df &= (3 - 1) \times (3 - 1) \\ df &= 2 \times 2 \\ df &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

This calculation is a key part of the process and often earns the first mark in the markscheme.

steps.title: Calculate the p-value
steps.content:
Using the observed frequencies from the table, we enter the data into a matrix on our GDC to perform a $\chi^2$ 2-way test. 

Based on the calculated statistic $\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 11.9$, the calculator provides the $p$-value:

$$p \approx 0.0181$$

4. Execute the test to find the p-value.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
In the **p-value comparison approach**, we compare our $p$-value to the significance level ($\alpha = 0.05$).

$$\begin{aligned} p \text{-value} &\approx 0.0181 \\ \alpha &= 0.05 \end{aligned}$$

Since $0.0181 < 0.05$, we **reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$)**.

steps.title: Final contextual conclusion
steps.content: Because we rejected $H_0$, we conclude that there is sufficient evidence at the $5\%$ significance level to suggest that **complaint type is not independent of the store**.

parts.content:
Calculate the probability that:
(i) a randomly selected complaint from Store $X$ is about delivery.
(ii) two randomly selected complaints from Store $X$ are both about quality, assuming independence between selections.
parts.markscheme:
(i) Total Store $X$ complaints $= 30 + 25 + 20 = 75$
$P(\text{delivery}) = \frac{20}{75} = \frac{4}{15} \approx 0.267$ <marks>A1</marks>

(ii) $P(\text{quality}) = \frac{30}{75} = 0.4$ <marks>A1</marks>
$P(\text{both quality}) = 0.4 \times 0.4 = 0.16$ <marks>M1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

parts.content:
The following table shows the store satisfaction scores and the number of service complaints for each store:

| Store | Satisfaction Score | Service Complaints |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $X$ | 85 | 25 |
| $Y$ | 78 | 40 |
| $Z$ | 82 | 35 |

Compute Spearman's rank correlation coefficient, $r_s$, for this data.
parts.markscheme:
Ranking the data (using 1 for highest score/frequency):
Satisfaction Score: $X: 1, Z: 2, Y: 3$ <marks>A1</marks>
Service Complaints: $Y: 1, Z: 2, X: 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

Differences ($d$): $X(1-3 = -2), Y(3-1 = 2), Z(2-2 = 0)$ <marks>A1</marks>
$\sum d^2 = (-2)^2 + 2^2 + 0^2 = 8$

$r_s = 1 - \frac{6\sum d^2}{n(n^2-1)} = 1 - \frac{6(8)}{3(3^2-1)}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$r_s = 1 - \frac{48}{24} = -1$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Interpret the value of $r_s$ in the context of the problem.
parts.markscheme:
There is a perfect negative correlation between satisfaction scores and service complaints. <marks>A1</marks>

This suggests that as the number of service complaints increases, the satisfaction score decreases. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the coefficient
steps.content: Spearman's rank correlation coefficient ($r_s$) measures the strength and direction of the relationship between two ranked variables. In this case, based on the markscheme, we are interpreting a value of $r_s = -1$.

steps.title: Determine strength and direction
steps.content:
A value of $-1$ indicates a **perfect negative correlation**. 

- **Perfect**: The relationship is entirely consistent across all data points.
- **Negative**: The variables move in opposite directions.

steps.title: Interpret in context
steps.content: In the context of this retailer, a perfect negative correlation means that as the number of **service complaints increases**, the **store satisfaction score decreases** in a perfectly predictable manner. This suggests an inverse relationship where more complaints directly correspond to lower customer satisfaction.

highlights.text: service complaints increases, the satisfaction score decreases

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must provide two specific points:
1. Identify the relationship as a **perfect negative correlation** (1 mark).
2. Describe the **inverse relationship** in context (as complaints go up, satisfaction goes down) (1 mark).

questions.specification:
A local café records the type of coffee (espresso, latte, or cappuccino) ordered by customers and whether they order food (yes or no) over 120 transactions. The data is summarized in the following table:

| | Food Ordered | No Food Ordered | Total |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Espresso | 15 | 25 | 40 |
| Latte | 20 | 30 | 50 |
| Cappuccino | 10 | 20 | 30 |
| **Total** | 45 | 75 | 120 |

The café manager conducts a $\chi^2$ test at the $5\%$ significance level to investigate whether the type of coffee ordered is independent of whether food is ordered.

The type of coffee ordered and whether food is ordered are independent. <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Accept "no association between coffee type and food order" or equivalent. Do not accept "not correlated" or "not related".

steps.title: Understand the chi-squared test
steps.content:
In statistics, a $\chi^2$ test for independence is used to determine if there is a relationship between two categorical variables. This topic is covered in **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability** of your syllabus. 

The first step in any hypothesis test is to state the null hypothesis, denoted as $H_0$.

steps.title: Identify the categorical variables
steps.content:
To write the null hypothesis, we first need to identify the two categorical variables being compared in the study. Looking at the table provided in the question, these variables are:

1. The **type of coffee ordered** (espresso, latte, or cappuccino)
2. **Whether food is ordered** (yes or no)

highlights.text: type of coffee (espresso, latte, or cappuccino) ordered by customers and whether they order food (yes or no)

steps.title: Formulate the independence statement
steps.content:
For a $\chi^2$ test of independence, the null hypothesis ($H_0$) always states that the two variables are **independent**. This means that the choice of one (like the type of coffee) does not affect the probability of the other (like ordering food).

We combine the variables into a clear statement of independence:

$$H_0: \text{The type of coffee ordered and whether food is ordered are independent.}$$

steps.title: Final check of requirements
steps.content:
When writing your answer, ensure you use the word "independent" or the phrase "no association." 

**Note:** Be careful not to use the terms "correlated" or "related," as these are generally reserved for numerical data (like Pearson's correlation) rather than categorical data in a $\chi^2$ test.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.content:
To write a null hypothesis ($H_0$) for a $\chi^2$ test of independence, we must first identify the two categorical variables being studied. Looking at the table, we are comparing:

1. The **type of coffee ordered** (Espresso, Latte, or Cappuccino).
2. **Whether food is ordered** (Yes or No).

This topic is part of **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability (SL 4.11)**, which covers how to test if two variables are linked or independent.

highlights.text: type of coffee (espresso, latte, or cappuccino) ordered
whether they order food (yes or no)

steps.title: State the No Association hypothesis
steps.content:
In statistics, the **null hypothesis** ($H_0$) is the "default" assumption that there is no special connection between the variables. 

Using the **No Association Statement** approach, we formulate $H_0$ by explicitly stating that there is no relationship between the choice of coffee and the decision to order food:

$$H_0: \text{There is no association between the type of coffee ordered and whether food is ordered.}$$

In an IB exam, you can also use the word "independent," but since we are using the **No Association** method, this specific phrasing is the most direct way to earn the mark.

highlights.text: no association between coffee type and food order

Total latte orders $= 20 + 30 = 50$, total food orders $= 15 + 20 + 10 = 45$. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Given that the $\chi^2$ test statistic is approximately $0.356$ with $2$ degrees of freedom, determine the conclusion of the test. Justify your answer using the critical value or $p$-value.
parts.markscheme:
Critical value for $\chi^2$ with $2$ df at $5\% = 5.991$ <marks>A1</marks>

Since $0.356 < 5.991$ (or $p\text{-value} \approx 0.837 > 0.05$), do not reject $H_0$. <marks>A1</marks>

There is insufficient evidence to suggest that the type of coffee ordered and whether food is ordered are dependent. (Accept: They are independent). <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Accept conclusion based on $p$-value $> 0.05$ if clearly stated.

steps.title: Identify the test parameters
steps.content:
To determine the conclusion of the $\chi^2$ test using the critical value approach, we first list the information provided in the question:

*   **Test Statistic ($\chi^2_{calc}$):** $0.356$
*   **Degrees of Freedom ($df$):** $2$
*   **Significance Level ($\alpha$):** $0.05$ (or $5\%$)

This question falls under **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability (SL 4.11)**, which covers the $\chi^2$ test for independence.

steps.title: Find the critical value
steps.content:
The critical value defines the boundary of the "rejection region." For a $\chi^2$ test, if the test statistic is greater than the critical value, we reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$).

Using a GDC, we find the critical value for a $\chi^2$ distribution with $df = 2$ and a significance level of $0.05$ (which corresponds to an area of $0.95$ to the left of the critical value).

$$\chi^2_{crit}(0.05, 2) \approx 5.991$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, choose Distributions, then Chi-squared, and select Inverse Chi-squared. Enter the area (0.95) and the degrees of freedom (2).

ti84.instructions: Go to [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR). Select 'invChi2('. Enter Area: 0.95 (this is 1 - 0.05), df: 2. Press Paste and Enter.

desmos.instructions: In the expression bar, type `inversecdf(chisquare(2), 0.95)`.

calculator.description: Calculate the critical value for a Chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom at the 5% level.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Now, we compare our calculated test statistic to the critical value:

$$\text{Test Statistic } (0.356) < \text{ Critical Value } (5.991)$$

Since the test statistic is **less than** the critical value, it does not fall into the rejection region. Therefore, we **do not reject** the null hypothesis ($H_0$).

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
In the context of the problem, $H_0$ states that the type of coffee and food ordering are independent. Since we do not reject $H_0$:

There is **insufficient evidence** to suggest that the type of coffee ordered and whether food is ordered are dependent. We conclude they are **independent**.

**Marks awarded:**
*   **A1** for correctly finding the critical value $5.991$.
*   **A1** for the comparison $0.356 < 5.991$ and stating "do not reject $H_0$".
*   **A1** for the contextual conclusion (independence).

steps.title: State the hypotheses
steps.content:
Before we interpret the results, we must clearly state our null hypothesis ($H_0$) and alternative hypothesis ($H_1$).

- $H_0$: The type of coffee ordered and whether food is ordered are **independent**.
- $H_1$: The type of coffee ordered and whether food is ordered are **dependent**.

highlights.text: whether the type of coffee ordered is independent of whether food is ordered

steps.title: Calculate the p-value
steps.content:
We are given a chi-squared test statistic ($\chi^2_{calc}$) of $0.356$ and $2$ degrees of freedom ($df$). We need to find the $p$-value, which represents the probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as $0.356$, assuming $H_0$ is true.

Using a GDC, we find:
$$p\text{-value} = P(\chi^2 > 0.356) \approx 0.837$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, then Dist, then Chi, then Ccd. Set lower as 0.356, upper as a very large number like 10^99, and df as 2.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTRIBUTION). Select '8: χ²cdf('. Enter lower bound: 0.356, upper bound: 1E99, df: 2. Press Paste and Enter.

desmos.instructions: Use the Chi-Squared Distribution function: `1 - chiSquareDist(2).cdf(0.356)`

calculator.description: Calculate the p-value for a Chi-squared distribution.

highlights.text: test statistic is approximately $0.356$ with $2$ degrees of freedom

steps.title: Compare with significance level
steps.content:
The significance level is $\alpha = 5\% = 0.05$. In a $p$-value comparison test, we use the following rule:
- If $p\text{-value} \leq \alpha$, reject $H_0$.
- If $p\text{-value} > \alpha$, do not reject $H_0$.

Comparing our values:
$$0.837 > 0.05$$

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since the $p$-value is significantly greater than $0.05$, we **do not reject** the null hypothesis.

**Conclusion:** There is insufficient evidence to suggest that the type of coffee ordered and whether food is ordered are dependent. We conclude that they are independent.

  • Calculate the probability: $0.212$ ($0.2118\dots$) A1

2 marks total

parts.content: Find the probability that two melons picked at random and independently will both have a mass greater than $3.0 \text{ kg}$.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiply probabilities for independent events: $0.2118\dots \times 0.2118\dots$ M1
  • Calculate the probability: $0.0449$ ($0.04488\dots$) A1

2 marks total

parts.content: Find the probability that two melons picked at random and independently have a total mass greater than $6.0 \text{ kg}$.
parts.markscheme:
• Let $T$ represent the total mass
  • State the mean of $T$: $\text{E}(T) = 5.2 \text{ kg}$ A1
  • Calculate the variance of $T$: $\text{Var}(T) = 0.5^2 + 0.5^2 = 0.5\text{ kg}^2$ M1 A1
  • Recognize distribution $T \sim \text{N}(5.2, 0.5)$, where the second parameter is the variance (in $\text{kg}^2$)
  • Calculate the probability: $P(T > 6.0) = 0.129$ ($0.1289\dots$) A1

4 marks total

parts.content:
One year, due to favourable weather conditions, it is thought that the mean mass of the melons has increased.

The owner of the farm decides to take a random sample of $16$ melons to test this hypothesis at the $5\%$ significance level, assuming the standard deviation of the masses of the melons has not changed.

Write down the null and alternative hypotheses for the test.
parts.markscheme:
• Let $\mu$ be the mean mass of the melons produced by the farm
  • State the hypotheses: $\text{H}_0: \mu = 2.6 \text{ kg}$, $\text{H}_1: \mu > 2.6 \text{ kg}$ A1

1 mark total

Accept equivalent text statements if $2.6 \text{ kg}$ is explicitly included.

parts.content: Find the critical region for this test.
parts.markscheme:
• Identify distribution of sample mean under $\text{H}_0$: $\bar{X} \sim \text{N}\left(2.6 \text{ kg}, \frac{0.5^2}{16}\text{ kg}^2\right)$ A1
  • Set up probability statement for critical region: $P(\bar{X} > a) = 0.05$ M1
  • Find the critical value: $a = 2.81 \text{ kg}$ ($2.8056\dots \text{ kg}$) A1
  • State the critical region: $\bar{X} > 2.81 \text{ kg}$ A1

4 marks total

parts.content:
Unknown to the farmer, the favourable weather conditions have led to all the melons having $10\%$ greater mass than the model described above.

Find the mean and standard deviation of the mass of the melons for this year.
parts.markscheme:
• Let $W$ represent the new mass of the melons
  • Calculate the new mean: $\text{E}(W) = 1.1 \times 2.6 = 2.86$ A1
  • Calculate the new standard deviation: $\text{SD}(W) = 1.1 \times 0.5$ M1
  • Obtain the standard deviation: $0.55$ A1

3 marks total

Award M1A0 for variance calculation error: $\text{Var}(W) = 1.1^2 \times 0.5^2 = 0.3025$.

parts.content: Find the probability of a Type II error in the owner’s test.
parts.markscheme:
• Identify definition of Type II error: $P(\text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \mid \text{H}_1 \text{ is true})$
  • Substitute values: $P\left(\bar{X} < 2.81 \mid \mu = 2.86, \sigma = \frac{0.55}{4}\right)$ M1
  • Calculate the probability: $0.346$ ($0.3462\dots$) A1

2 marks total

Accept $0.358$ from use of the three-figure answer to part (e).
